text:
Admission Date:  1991-01-01              Discharge Date:   1992-12-17

Date of Birth:  2000-09-06             Sex:   M

Service: NEUROLOGY

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:James
Chief Complaint:
"I can't move my legs"

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None.

History of Present Illness:
The patient is a 20 year old man with no significant past
medical
history now presenting with acute onset of weakness and
paraplegia.  The patient's symptoms began about a week ago with
a sore throat on that Sunday.  That next Monday, he felt ill all
day and slept most of the day.  On Tuesday, he went to his
doctor's office, they performed a rapid strep test which was
negative.  His symptoms persisted and he presented to an OSH on
Thursday where they prescribed him an antibiotic and discharged
him from the ED.  On that Saturday evening, he went to a friends
house to watch tv.  On returning late that night, he had great
difficulty climbing the stairs to his bedroom.  It took him 10
minutes when it normally takes 10 seconds.  He went to bed that
night and awoke at 4 am with a burning like pain in his thighs.
He had never experienced pain like this before.  He tried to get
out of bed, but his legs wouldn't move and he collapsed onto the
floor next to his bed.  He also reports inability to urinate,
but
no apparent episode of incontinence.

The patient was noted to be paraplegic in the ER, given ativan,
and admitted to the NICU for close monitoring.


Past Medical History:
GERD

Social History:
-Lives with grandparents
-No tobacco and social ETOH use
-No illicit drug use
-Works in customer service


Family History:
-No family history of strokes or auto-immune diseases


Physical Exam:
Vitals: 98.4  130/80  90  18  100% RA

General: Well nourished, in no acute distress
Neck: supple
Lungs: Clear to auscultation
CV: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs
Abdomen:  non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present
Ext: warm, no edema, good pulses

Neurologic Examination:
Mental Status: Awake and alert, cooperative with exam, normal
affect
Oriented to person, place, month and president
Attention: Can say months of year backward and forward
Language: Fluent, no dysarthria, no paraphasic errors, naming
intact
Fund of knowledge normal
Registration: 07-24 items, Recall 07-24 items at 3 minutes
No apraxia, No neglect
3375 Jeffrey Meadows
Amychester, NJ 38480 and writing intact

Cranial Nerves: Visual fields are full to confrontation. Pupils
equally round and reactive to light, 4 to 2 mm bilaterally.
Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. Facial sensation and
facial movement normal bilaterally. Hearing intact to finger rub
bilaterally. Tongue midline, no fasciculations.
Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius normal bilaterally.

Motor:
Normal bulk and tone bilaterally
No tremor.

      D T B WF WE FiF Rogers IP Gl Q H AF AE TF TE
Right 5 5 5 5  5  4+  4   0  0  0 0 0  0  0  0
Left  5 5 5 5  5  5   5-  0  0  0 0 0  0  0  0

No pronator drift

Sensation was diminished to Light touch, pin prick, temperature
(cold), vibration, and proprioception at a level right at the
thoracic/abdomen border T7

Reflexes: B T Br Pa Pl
Right     1 1 1  0  0
Left      1 1 1  0  0

Grasp reflex absent

Toes were mute

Coordination is ataxic on finger-nose-finger on right, rapid
alternating movements slowed on right

Gait not testable


Pertinent Results:
Cbc: 6.1/39.3/221 mcv=86
Chem: 140/4.0  106/23  9/0.8  98
Coags: 12.8/28.9/1.1
CSF:  wbc=247 (39 polys, 51 lymphs); rbc=143

Spine MR:  abnormal enhancement of the thoracic cord with cord
expansion

Brain MR:  Multiple foci of abnormal signal within the brain and
especially in white matter tracts is consistent with a
demyelinating process. No abnormal enhancement is identified.

1991-01-01 02:36PM   SED RATE-35*
1991-01-01 02:36PM   Joshua-NEGATIVE
1991-01-01 02:36PM   SED RATE-35*
1991-01-01 02:36PM   LUPUS-NEG

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to NICU for close monitoring.  He was
begun on IV steroids (1g of solumedrol qd) in addition to
empiric treatment with ceftriaxone and gangcyclovir.  An MR of
his brain showed demyelinating lesions similar to those seen in
his spinal cord and the working diagnosis was a transverse
myelitis secondary to some likely post-infectious autoimmune
etiology.  His NIFs and VC were monitored and were always within
normal range.  The patients neuro exam on the day of discharge
was significant still for bilateral leg paralysis and a T6
sensory level.  He remains afebrile, alert and with stable vital
signs.

Medications on Admission:
None

Discharge Medications:
heparin 500 tid
insulin sc
solumedrol 1g qd for one more day then prednisone 60 qd for 6
months
protonix 40 qd


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Fleming County Hospital - 28466 Jeffery Mission Suite 502
Rebeccamouth, NV 36840

Discharge Diagnosis:
1. transverse myelitis
2. insomnia

Discharge Condition:
Stable, still with paraplegia and T6 sensory level.

Discharge Instructions:
The patient will need to continue IV steroids for one more day
and then switch to PO steroids (prednisone 60 mg) for at least 6
months.

The patient should continue to have his vital capacity and NIF's
checked daily.

The patient will need aggressive PT and OT.

Followup Instructions:
The patient should follow up with Dr. Daniel Salinas in Neurology
resident clinic in about 3 months, an appointment will be made
for you.


Completed by:1992-12-17
